categories.name: IGBT Modules
categories.description: Starpower IGBT modules feature advanced trench field-stop technology with voltage ratings from 600V to 1700V. These modules offer high power density, low switching losses, and excellent thermal performance for industrial drives, EVs, and renewable energy applications.
categories.longDescription: Starpower's IGBT module portfolio represents the company's core competency in high-power semiconductor packaging. The modules utilize advanced trench field-stop IGBT chips combined with optimized freewheeling diodes to deliver industry-leading performance. Voltage ratings span from 600V for industrial drives to 1700V for high-power renewable energy and traction applications. Current ratings range from tens to hundreds of amperes, accommodating applications from small servo drives to large MW-scale inverters. Starpower's proprietary packaging technology ensures excellent thermal management, low parasitic inductance, and high reliability under demanding operating conditions. All modules undergo comprehensive testing including static parameters, switching characteristics, and thermal cycling. As an authorized distributor, BeiLuo provides technical support for module selection, thermal design, and application optimization. As an authorized distributor, we provide comprehensive IGBT Modules selection guidance (选型指导) and technical support.

faqs.question: What voltage ratings are available for Starpower IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower IGBT modules are available in three main voltage ratings: (1) 600V-650V series for industrial motor drives, servo systems, and power supplies with input voltages up to 400VAC. (2) 1200V series for electric vehicle powertrains, solar inverters, and 690VAC industrial drives. (3) 1700V series for high-voltage applications including wind power converters, traction drives, and medium-voltage drives. The voltage rating should be selected based on the DC bus voltage with adequate margin (typically 1.5-2x) for voltage transients and switching spikes. Starpower provides detailed voltage derating curves for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for voltage rating selection based on your specific application requirements.

faqs.question: What packages are available for Starpower IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower IGBT modules are available in industry-standard packages: (1) 34mm and 62mm half-bridge modules - standard industrial packages compatible with major manufacturers. (2) EasyPIM and EasyPACK - compact modules with integrated rectifier and brake chopper. (3) EconoDUAL and EconoPACK - high-power modules for industrial drives and renewable energy. (4) Custom packages - specialized modules for automotive and traction applications. All packages feature low-inductance design, direct bonded copper (DBC) substrates, and optimized thermal interfaces. Package selection depends on power rating, cooling method, and mechanical mounting requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for package recommendations based on your thermal and mechanical requirements.

faqs.question: How do Starpower IGBT modules compare to international brands?
faqs.answer: Starpower IGBT modules offer competitive performance compared to international brands: (1) Electrical performance - comparable VCE(sat), switching losses, and SOA characteristics. (2) Thermal performance - advanced DBC substrates and thermal interface materials provide excellent heat dissipation. (3) Reliability - extensive qualification testing including temperature cycling, power cycling, and HTRB. (4) Cost - typically 20-30% more cost-effective than equivalent international brands. (5) Supply - shorter lead times and better availability for Asia-Pacific customers. Starpower modules have been qualified by major industrial and automotive OEMs, proving field reliability in millions of installations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Request a detailed comparison report or samples to evaluate Starpower modules for your application.

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate drive voltage for GD50HFL120C1S?
faqs.answer: GD50HFL120C1S is optimized for +15V turn-on and -8V turn-off gate drive: (1) +15V VGE(on) ensures low VCE(sat) and minimizes conduction losses. (2) -8V negative turn-off voltage prevents false turn-on due to dv/dt and Miller effect. (3) Gate resistor selection affects switching speed and EMI - typical values are 15-33Ω for turn-on and 10-22Ω for turn-off. The gate drive should provide 2-4A peak current for fast switching. Starpower provides recommended gate drive circuits and PCB layout guidelines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for gate driver IC recommendations and gate drive circuit design.

faqs.question: What is the thermal resistance of GD50HFL120C1S?
faqs.answer: GD50HFL120C1S in 34mm package has the following thermal characteristics: (1) Junction-to-case thermal resistance RthJC = 0.65K/W (IGBT) and 1.2K/W (diode). (2) Maximum junction temperature Tj(max) = 150°C. (3) Recommended operating junction temperature Tj(op) = -40°C to +125°C. For continuous operation at 50A with 1.7V VCE(sat), the conduction loss is 85W, requiring a heatsink with thermal resistance of approximately 0.5K/W or better to maintain junction temperature below 125°C at 40°C ambient. Thermal interface material with low thermal resistance (0.1-0.2K·cm²/W) is recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for thermal simulation and heatsink selection guidance.

faqs.question: Can GD50HFL120C1S be used for servo drive applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, GD50HFL120C1S is well-suited for servo drive applications. Its characteristics make it ideal for this demanding application: (1) 1200V rating provides adequate margin for 380-480VAC servo systems. (2) 50A current rating supports servo drives up to 15-20kW. (3) Fast switching with controlled dv/dt reduces motor insulation stress. (4) Low VCE(sat) minimizes losses during frequent acceleration/deceleration cycles. (5) 10μs short-circuit withstand provides reliable protection during output faults. The module's compact 34mm package fits standard servo drive mechanical designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for servo drive design support and PWM frequency optimization.

faqs.question: What is the short-circuit withstand time of GD50HFL120C1S?
faqs.answer: GD50HFL120C1S has a short-circuit withstand time (tSC) of 10μs minimum at VGE = 15V, VCC = 800V, and starting Tj = 125°C. This specification ensures reliable protection during output short-circuit conditions. The IGBT desaturation detection circuit should detect the fault and turn off the IGBT within 10μs to prevent device damage. Starpower recommends using gate drivers with integrated desaturation protection for reliable short-circuit protection. The module has been tested for repetitive short-circuit robustness under specified conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for short-circuit protection circuit design and desaturation detection implementation.

faqs.question: What is the diode recovery characteristics of the built-in anti-parallel diode?
faqs.answer: GD50HFL120C1S's built-in anti-parallel diode features: (1) Fast reverse recovery time trr = 120ns typical at IF = 25A. (2) Soft recovery characteristics to minimize voltage overshoot and EMI. (3) Low forward voltage VF = 1.8V typical at IF = 25A. (4) Reverse recovery charge Qrr = 1.2μC typical. These characteristics make the diode suitable for high-frequency freewheeling applications in inverter bridges. The soft recovery minimizes dv/dt stress on the complementary IGBT and reduces EMI filtering requirements. The diode is rated for the same current as the IGBT for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for diode recovery characteristics data and snubber circuit design.

faqs.question: Is GD50HFL120C1S suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: GD50HFL120C1S is built on an industrial-grade platform. For automotive applications, Starpower offers AEC-Q101 qualified versions with enhanced reliability testing. The automotive versions undergo additional testing including: (1) Temperature cycling (-40°C to +150°C). (2) High-temperature operating life (HTOL) at 150°C. (3) Power cycling for package reliability. (4) ESD testing per AEC-Q101. For automotive applications such as EV auxiliary drives, HVAC compressors, and DC-DC converters, the automotive-qualified versions are recommended. Contact Starpower for automotive qualification reports and PPAP documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for automotive-qualified module recommendations and qualification data.

categories.name: SiC Modules
categories.description: Starpower SiC modules feature silicon carbide MOSFETs and Schottky diodes for high-frequency, high-efficiency power conversion. These modules enable system-level benefits including reduced cooling requirements, smaller passive components, and higher power density.
categories.longDescription: Starpower's SiC module portfolio represents the cutting edge of power semiconductor technology. Silicon carbide (SiC) offers superior material properties compared to silicon, including higher breakdown voltage, lower on-resistance, and faster switching speeds. Starpower's SiC modules combine these benefits with robust packaging technology to deliver unmatched performance in demanding applications. The modules feature SiC MOSFETs with ultra-low RDS(on) and SiC Schottky diodes with zero reverse recovery, enabling switching frequencies up to 100kHz. This allows for dramatic reductions in passive component size, filter weight, and cooling system requirements. Applications include EV charging infrastructure, solar inverters, and high-frequency power supplies where efficiency and power density are critical. As an authorized distributor, BeiLuo provides technical support for SiC module selection, gate driver design, and application optimization. As an authorized distributor, we provide comprehensive SiC Modules selection guidance (选型指导) and technical support.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of Starpower SiC modules over IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower SiC modules offer significant advantages over IGBT modules: (1) Higher switching frequency - SiC can switch at 50-100kHz vs 10-20kHz for IGBT, enabling smaller filters and magnetics. (2) Lower switching losses - SiC has negligible switching losses, improving efficiency especially at light loads. (3) Lower conduction losses - SiC MOSFETs have lower RDS(on) temperature coefficient. (4) No tail current - SiC MOSFETs have no tail current during turn-off, reducing switching losses. (5) Higher efficiency - typically 1-3% higher system efficiency. (6) Reduced cooling - lower losses mean smaller heatsinks and cooling systems. The trade-off is higher cost, but system-level savings often justify the premium.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for SiC vs IGBT comparison and application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What gate drive requirements do Starpower SiC modules have?
faqs.answer: Starpower SiC modules have specific gate drive requirements: (1) Gate voltage - typically +15V to +18V for turn-on and -3V to -5V for turn-off. The negative turn-off voltage is important to prevent false turn-on due to high dv/dt. (2) Gate resistance - lower than IGBT, typically 5-15Ω, to achieve fast switching. (3) Gate driver capability - 5-10A peak current for fast charging/discharging of gate capacitance. (4) Isolation - reinforced isolation required due to high dv/dt (up to 100V/ns). (5) Protection - active Miller clamp recommended for high dv/dt immunity. Starpower provides recommended gate driver ICs and reference circuits for their SiC modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for gate driver recommendations and circuit design guidelines.

faqs.question: What applications benefit most from Starpower SiC modules?
faqs.answer: Applications that benefit most from Starpower SiC modules include: (1) EV charging - high-frequency operation enables compact designs for DC fast chargers. (2) Solar inverters - higher switching frequency reduces filter size and weight. (3) High-frequency power supplies - enables MHz-level switching for ultra-compact designs. (4) Automotive DC-DC converters - high efficiency and power density for onboard converters. (5) Industrial drives - higher switching frequency reduces motor current ripple and audible noise. (6) Energy storage systems - bidirectional operation with high efficiency. The key criteria are applications where switching frequency, efficiency, or power density is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for application suitability assessment and ROI analysis.

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate drive voltage for GDS100M12B1?
faqs.answer: GDS100M12B1 is optimized for +18V turn-on and -3V to -5V turn-off gate drive: (1) +18V VGS(on) ensures low RDS(on) and minimizes conduction losses. (2) Negative turn-off voltage (-3V to -5V) is critical to prevent false turn-on due to high dv/dt (>50V/ns). (3) Gate resistor selection affects switching speed and EMI - typical values are 5-10Ω for both turn-on and turn-off. (4) The gate drive should provide 5-10A peak current for fast switching. (5) Active Miller clamp is recommended for high dv/dt immunity. Starpower provides detailed gate drive recommendations and reference circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for SiC gate driver IC recommendations and circuit design.

faqs.question: What is the thermal resistance of GDS100M12B1?
faqs.answer: GDS100M12B1 in Easy1B package has the following thermal characteristics: (1) Junction-to-case thermal resistance RthJC = 0.4K/W. (2) Maximum junction temperature Tj(max) = 175°C. (3) Recommended operating junction temperature Tj(op) = -40°C to +150°C. For continuous operation at 100A with 12mΩ RDS(on), the conduction loss is 120W at 150°C (higher than 25°C due to positive temperature coefficient). This requires a heatsink with thermal resistance of approximately 0.3K/W or better to maintain junction temperature below 150°C at 40°C ambient. High-performance thermal interface material is recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for thermal simulation and heatsink selection guidance.

faqs.question: Can GDS100M12B1 be used for EV fast charging applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, GDS100M12B1 is ideal for EV fast charging applications. Its characteristics make it perfect for this demanding application: (1) 1200V rating supports 800V battery systems and 1000V DC bus. (2) 100A current rating supports DC fast chargers up to 100-150kW. (3) 100kHz switching enables compact magnetic components. (4) High efficiency (>98%) reduces cooling requirements. (5) Bidirectional capability supports V2G applications. (6) High reliability for 24/7 operation. The Easy1B package is widely used in EV charger designs and compatible with standard heatsinks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for EV charger design support and topology recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the switching performance of GDS100M12B1 compared to IGBT?
faqs.answer: GDS100M12B1 offers dramatically superior switching performance compared to IGBT: (1) Switching frequency - up to 100kHz vs 10-20kHz for IGBT. (2) Turn-on time - typically 50ns vs 200-500ns for IGBT. (3) Turn-off time - typically 30ns vs 300-800ns for IGBT (including tail). (4) Switching losses - approximately 1/10th of equivalent IGBT. (5) dv/dt - up to 100V/ns vs 5-10V/ns for IGBT. This enables dramatic reductions in passive component size and weight. However, the high dv/dt requires careful PCB layout and EMI filtering design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for switching performance analysis and EMI mitigation strategies.

faqs.question: What are the layout considerations for GDS100M12B1?
faqs.answer: GDS100M12B1 requires careful PCB layout due to high switching speed: (1) Minimize gate loop inductance - use wide, short traces and place gate driver close to module. (2) Kelvin source connection - use separate source connection for gate drive to avoid source inductance feedback. (3) Decoupling capacitors - place high-frequency ceramic capacitors (100nF-1μF) very close to module terminals. (4) Minimize power loop inductance - use laminated busbars or parallel planes for DC link. (5) EMI considerations - implement proper shielding and filtering due to high dv/dt. (6) Thermal vias - use sufficient thermal vias under the module for heat dissipation. Starpower provides detailed layout guidelines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for PCB layout review and optimization recommendations.

faqs.question: How does the cost of GDS100M12B1 compare to IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: GDS100M12B1 has higher component cost than equivalent IGBT modules (typically 2-3x), but system-level cost may be lower: (1) Passive components - smaller filters and magnetics due to high frequency (30-50% savings). (2) Cooling - smaller heatsinks and fans due to higher efficiency (20-30% savings). (3) Enclosure - smaller size and weight reduce enclosure cost. (4) Energy savings - 1-3% higher efficiency reduces operating costs. (5) Reliability - lower operating temperatures improve system reliability. For high-volume applications, the total cost of ownership often favors SiC. Starpower can provide ROI analysis for specific applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for system cost analysis and ROI calculations.

Its characteristics make it ideal for this demanding application: (1) 1200V rating supports 800V EV platforms and provides margin for load dump. faqs.question: What is the difference between GDS50M12B1 and GDS100M12B1?
faqs.answer: GDS50M12B1 and GDS100M12B1 serve different current levels: (1) GDS50M12B1 (50A, 25mΩ) is designed for lower current applications (10-25kW) with higher RDS(on) but lower cost. (2) GDS100M12B1 (100A, 12mΩ) is for higher current applications (25-50kW) with lower RDS(on) and better thermal performance. Both feature the same Easy1B package and 1200V rating. GDS50M12B1 requires less gate charge (lower gate driver power) and may be sufficient for many applications. For parallel operation, GDS100M12B1 is preferred due to better current sharing. Selection depends on current requirements and cost optimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for SiC module selection based on your specific current and efficiency requirements.

faqs.question: What is the short-circuit capability of GDS50M12B1?
faqs.answer: GDS50M12B1 has limited short-circuit withstand capability compared to IGBT: (1) Short-circuit withstand time is typically 2-5μs vs 10μs for IGBT. (2) This is due to higher current density and faster thermal runaway in SiC. (3) Fast desaturation detection (<2μs) is critical for protection. (4) Soft turn-off is recommended to avoid overvoltage spikes. (5) Current limiting during startup and fault conditions is important. Starpower provides recommended protection circuits and desaturation detection guidelines. The fast switching of SiC actually helps in quickly limiting fault current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for short-circuit protection design and desaturation detection circuits.

faqs.question: What are the EMI considerations for GDS50M12B1 designs?
faqs.answer: GDS50M12B1 requires careful EMI management due to high dv/dt: (1) dv/dt can exceed 50V/ns, generating high-frequency EMI. (2) Common mode currents through parasitic capacitances can cause EMI issues. (3) Proper shielding and grounding are essential. (4) EMI filters need to be designed for higher frequency content. (5) Snubber circuits may be needed to reduce voltage overshoot. (6) PCB layout should minimize loop areas and use proper grounding. Starpower provides EMI testing data and mitigation recommendations. The benefits of SiC often outweigh the EMI challenges with proper design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for EMI analysis and filter design support.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of Starpower MOSFET modules over discrete MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Starpower MOSFET modules offer several advantages over discrete MOSFETs: (1) Higher current capability - modules can handle hundreds of amperes vs tens of amperes for discrete devices. (2) Better thermal performance - modules use DBC substrates and advanced thermal interface materials for efficient heat dissipation. (3) Lower parasitic inductance - integrated design reduces stray inductance for cleaner switching. (4) Easier assembly - modules simplify mechanical mounting and electrical connections. (5) Higher reliability - qualified modules undergo extensive testing for industrial applications. (6) Compact design - modules achieve higher power density than discrete solutions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for module vs discrete selection based on your application requirements.

faqs.question: What voltage ratings are available for Starpower MOSFET modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower MOSFET modules are available in voltage ratings from 60V to 200V: (1) 60V-100V series for low-voltage high-current applications such as 48V battery systems, DC-DC converters, and low-voltage motor drives. (2) 150V-200V series for medium-voltage applications including 110V/220V industrial drives and power supplies. The voltage rating should be selected with adequate margin (typically 1.3-1.5x) for voltage transients and switching spikes. Lower voltage ratings offer lower RDS(on) for the same die size, optimizing conduction losses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for voltage rating selection based on your specific application requirements.

faqs.question: What applications are best suited for Starpower MOSFET modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower MOSFET modules are ideal for high-current switching applications: (1) DC-DC converters - high-frequency buck/boost converters for battery systems and power supplies. (2) Motor drives - low-voltage high-current motor control for EVs and industrial applications. (3) Power distribution - solid-state switches and circuit breakers for high-current distribution. (4) Battery management - high-current battery switches and protection circuits. (5) Welding equipment - high-current switching for welding power supplies. (6) Induction heating - high-frequency switching for heating applications. The key criteria are high current (>50A) and low voltage (<200V).
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for application suitability assessment and module recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between diode and thyristor rectifier modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower offers both diode and thyristor rectifier modules: (1) Diode modules - uncontrolled rectification with fixed output voltage determined by input AC voltage. Simple, reliable, and cost-effective for applications not requiring voltage control. (2) Thyristor (SCR) modules - phase-controlled rectification allowing output voltage adjustment by controlling firing angle. Used for soft starters, DC motor speed control, and power regulation. Thyristors can handle high surge currents and are robust for industrial applications. Selection depends on whether voltage control is required. Both types offer high current capability and low forward voltage drop.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for diode vs thyristor selection based on your application requirements.

faqs.question: What voltage ratings are available for Starpower rectifier modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower rectifier modules are available in a wide range of voltage ratings: (1) 800V-1200V for standard industrial applications with 380-480VAC input. (2) 1600V-2200V for medium-voltage applications and higher safety margins. (3) 3000V-6000V for high-voltage applications such as traction drives and power transmission. The voltage rating should be selected based on the peak reverse voltage (PRV) requirement with adequate margin (typically 2-3x) for voltage transients and line surges. Higher voltage ratings provide better reliability but may have slightly higher forward voltage drop.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for voltage rating selection based on your specific application requirements.

faqs.question: What applications are best suited for Starpower rectifier modules?
faqs.answer: Starpower rectifier modules are ideal for high-current AC-DC power conversion: (1) Welding equipment - high-current rectification for arc welding power supplies. (2) Electroplating - controlled DC power for plating baths requiring high current at low voltage. (3) DC motor drives - rectification and control for DC motor speed regulation. (4) Battery chargers - high-current charging for industrial battery systems. (5) Induction heating - rectification for high-frequency heating power supplies. (6) Power supplies - AC-DC conversion for industrial DC power distribution. The key criteria are high current (>100A) and need for reliable, efficient rectification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for application suitability assessment and rectifier recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the reverse recovery time of GDR200A12?
faqs.answer: GDR200A12 has a fast reverse recovery time of 200ns typical at IF = 200A and di/dt = 200A/μs. The reverse recovery charge Qrr is approximately 40μC. Fast reverse recovery is important for high-frequency rectification and reduces switching losses in applications with rapid current commutation. The soft recovery characteristics minimize voltage overshoot and EMI. For standard 50/60Hz rectification, reverse recovery is not critical, but for high-frequency applications such as welding inverters, fast recovery reduces losses and improves efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for reverse recovery characteristics data and high-frequency rectification design.

faqs.question: How does GDR200A12 compare to thyristor modules for rectification?
faqs.answer: GDR200A12 (diode) and thyristor modules serve different rectification needs: (1) GDR200A12 provides uncontrolled rectification with fixed output voltage determined by AC input. Simple and reliable but no voltage control. (2) Thyristor modules provide phase-controlled rectification allowing output voltage adjustment. More complex control but enables soft-start and voltage regulation. Diodes have lower forward voltage drop and are more efficient for simple rectification. Thyristors are needed when voltage control or soft-start is required. For welding applications, diode bridges are often preferred for simplicity, while thyristors are used when current control is needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for diode vs thyristor selection for your specific rectification requirements.

(2) Repetitive surge current capability depends on duty cycle and cooling. (3) I²t rating = 20,000 A²s for fuse coordination. This high surge capability ensures the module can withstand inrush currents during startup, capacitor charging, and fault conditions. For applications with frequent high-current transients, proper heatsinking and thermal management are essential to prevent junction temperature from exceeding limits during surge events.

faqs.question: How does GDT100A16 compare to GDR200A12 for rectification?

faqs.question: What commutation considerations are needed for GDT100A16?

(3) For line-commutated applications (50/60Hz), natural commutation from AC line is sufficient. (4) For inverter applications, forced commutation or load commutation is required.
